input: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?
debate_transcript: [Ethical_Philosopher]: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?

[Causal_Scientist]: That's an interesting scenario, but I'd argue that causality isn't quite as straightforward here. The motion detector was triggered by Suzy and Billy both arriving at the same time, which is a coincidence rather than a causal relationship between their actions. If we consider the chain of cause-and-effect, it's not clear what specific action or event caused the motion detector to go off. Perhaps it was simply the simultaneous arrival of two people in the room, rather than any deliberate action by Billy.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I don't think that's a fair interpretation. If Suzy and Billy both arrive at exactly 9 am, it's not just a coincidence - they're following the same instructions from their boss. They're essentially doing what they were told to do. It feels like Billy is somehow responsible for triggering the motion detector because he's part of the team that was supposed to be there.

[Causal_Scientist]: That's a valid point, but I'd still argue that causality isn't quite so clear-cut. Just because Suzy and Billy are following the same instructions doesn't mean that one person's action directly causes the motion detector to go off. The motion detector was triggered by the simultaneous arrival of two people in the room, which is a joint event rather than a causal chain. It's not like Billy's presence alone caused the motion detector to trigger - it's more like the combination of Suzy and Billy's actions (or lack thereof) led to the motion detector going off. We need to be careful not to attribute causality where none exists, especially when dealing with complex systems like this.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I see what you're getting at, Causal_Scientist, but I'm still inclined to think that Billy's presence is somehow responsible for triggering the motion detector. The fact that Suzy and Billy are following the same instructions from their boss creates a kind of 'joint intentionality' - they're both acting in accordance with the same goal or plan, even if it's not a deliberate attempt to trigger the motion detector. This makes me wonder whether we should consider Billy's actions as part of a larger web of intentions and causality that goes beyond individual agency.
